title: Block Permutation Routing on Ramanujan Hypergraphs for Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computing
abstract: We analyze permutation routing of rigid blocks representing surface code patches of $d_C^2$ atoms on a reconfigurable lattice with hypergraph transformations. For a hypergraph $H$, code distance $d_C$, $s=d_C^2$, number of blocks $N_L$, and guard distance $g$, we show the block routing number $\mathrm{rt}_B(H, s, g) = Θ(d_C \log N_L)$. A spectral analysis of the quotient graph $Q(G_{\mathrm{cl}}(H), B)$ (blocks as supervertices) shows that the spectral ratio $β_Q < 1$ is preserved in the high-connectivity regime. Negative association of block permutations and congestion bounds are used for random intermediate configurations. Serialization establishes that each quotient routing phase requires $O(d_C)$ physical sub-steps due to the block footprint width. A lower bound $\mathrm{rt}_B = Ω(d_C \log N_L)$ follows from combining the spectral lower bound on quotient phases with the traversal cost per phase. We include error model analysis grounded in recent experimental results, syndrome extraction protocols (stop-and-correct, rolling active fault-tolerant (AFT) measurement, and adaptive deformation), and integration with lattice surgery compilation via the Litinski protocol. Composition with the correlated-decoding scheme reduces syndrome-extraction overhead from $O(d_C)$ to $O(1)$ per correction window, leaving routing as the leading-order contributor to the integrated $O(d_C \log N_L)$ depth. Spectral inheritance is organized in a hierarchy: exact (Haemers interlacing on equitable partitions), perturbative (Weyl bounds for near-equitable partitions, a practically relevant case for surface-code patches), and universal (higher-order Cheeger). Methods extend directly to QCCD trapped-ion architectures under the same regime condition, with junction crossings replacing AOD transports as the elementary single-hop translation.
summary: This paper provides a theoretical framework for efficient qubit routing in next-generation quantum computers. It proves that using high-connectivity Ramanujan hypergraphs allows for moving large error-correcting code patches with a routing depth that scales linearly with the code distance, significantly reducing the overhead for fault-tolerant operations.
main_problem: Determining the routing complexity and depth required to move large, rigid blocks of qubits (representing surface code patches) across reconfigurable quantum architectures without violating fault-tolerance constraints.
main_result: The authors establish that the block routing number scales as Theta(d_C log N_L), proving that routing depth scales linearly with code distance rather than quadratically, provided the hardware operates in a high-connectivity regime.
method: The study employs spectral graph theory, specifically using Haemers interlacing, Weyl perturbation, and higher-order Cheeger inequalities on Ramanujan hypergraphs and their quotient graphs, combined with LMR scheduling and Valiant's routing algorithm.
model_or_system: The model consists of surface code patches of size d_C^2 atoms moving as rigid blocks on a reconfigurable lattice modeled by the clique expansion of a Ramanujan hypergraph, applicable to neutral-atom and QCCD trapped-ion architectures.
key_observables: Block routing number (rt_B), routing depth, congestion, and spectral ratio (beta_Q).
important_parameters: Code distance (d_C), number of blocks (N_L), hypergraph rank (r), base degree (d), and the high-connectivity regime condition.
main_assumptions: The blocks are assumed to be rigid during transport, and the hardware must satisfy a high-connectivity threshold to maintain the expansion properties of the host graph.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the mapping from physical graphs to weighted quotient graphs; Figure 2 illustrates the three-layer spectral argument hierarchy; Table 2 compares simulated routing depth against theoretical predictions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the routing problem, defines the Ramanujan hypergraph model, develops a three-layer spectral argument for quotient graph inheritance, establishes the routing complexity bounds, integrates error models and syndrome extraction protocols, and concludes with extensions to QCCD and deformable patches.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Quotient graph construction. Left: the physical graph G = Gcl(H) with four blocks B1, . . . , B4 (colored rectangles), each containing d2 C atoms (dots), separated by guard distance g. Unoccupied vertices are shown in gray. Right: the (weighted) quotient graph Q(G, B), with one supervertex per block and edge weight AQ[i, j] equal to the average per-vertex inter-block edge count from Bi to Bj (Definition 2.8). Edge thickness is proportional to AQ[i, j]; only edges with AQ[i, j] > 0 are drawn. The spectral ratio βQ = λ∗(AQ)/d′ Q < 1 controls expansion.

Figure 2: Three-layer spectral argument for quotient graph expansion. Layer 1 (Theorem 4.4) gives the tightest bound with Haemers interlacing but requires an exactly equitable partition. Layer 2 (Theorem 4.11) handles near- equitable partitions with Weyl perturbation bounds. Layer 3 (Theorem 4.12) applies universally by higher-order Cheeger inequalities but gives the weakest bound. All three layers require (2) as an architectural prerequisite; the layers differ in the partition-structure assumption on B.

Figure 3: Numerical validation of the Θ(dC log NL) scaling. (a) Simulated routing depth Tsim versus the theoretical prediction dC log2 NL for dC ∈{3, 5, 7} and NL ∈{8, 16, 32, 64}. The dashed line is T = dC log2 NL; all points lie at or below this line, confirming the upper bound. (b) Normalized ratio α = Tsim/(dC log2 NL) versus NL. The ratio is bounded away from zero and bounded above by 1, consistent with the tight Θ(·) bound.
